title: Acne Vulgaris & Rosacea
oneLiner: Common inflammatory facial dermatoses; acne (young, comedonal, androgen-driven), rosacea (middle-aged, flushing, no comedones) — both cause scarring/psychosocial impact.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **No immediately life‑threatening mimic in typical acne or rosacea.**  
  However, ensure you have excluded:
  - **Perioral dermatitis** (steroid‑induced rosacea‑like eruption) — key finding: perioral distribution with sparing of vermillion border; history of topical steroid use.
  - **Carcinoid flush** — key finding: episodic facial flushing + diarrhoea/wheezing; check urinary 5‑HIAA if strong suspicion.
  - **Ocular rosacea keratitis** (can cause corneal ulceration/perforation) — key finding: eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision; urgent ophthalmology referral.
- **Severe acne fulminans** (acne with fever, arthralgia, bone pain, ulcerative lesions) — not fatal acutely, but can cause rapid scarring and systemic illness; requires immediate dermatology review.
sections.Presentation:
- **Acne vulgaris:**
  - Comedones (open blackheads, closed whiteheads) — pathognomonic for acne
  - Papules, pustules, nodules, cysts, scars
  - Distribution: face (cheeks, forehead, chin), chest, shoulders, back
  - Severity graded by number/type of lesions (mild: ≤20 comedones, few papules; moderate: 20–100 comedones, <50 papules/pustules; severe: nodules/cysts, scarring)
- **Rosacea:**
  - Persistent erythema + telangiectasias (central face: cheeks, nose, chin, forehead)
  - Papules and pustules (no comedones) — key differentiator from acne
  - Flushing/redness triggered by heat, alcohol, spicy foods, sunlight
  - Ocular symptoms: gritty eyes, blepharitis, conjunctivitis, styes
  - Phymatous changes: thickened skin (esp. rhinophyma) in long‑standing disease
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Acne vs rosacea** → comedones are **the splitter** (present in acne, absent in rosacea)
- **Perioral dermatitis** — perioral location, fine papules/pustules, often with steroid misuse history
- **Folliculitis** — follicular papules/pustules, may be bacterial (Staph) or fungal (Malassezia); no comedones, no facial flushing
- **Drug‑induced acneiform eruption** — sudden onset, monomorphic papules/pustules, history of corticosteroids, lithium, isoniazid, EGFR inhibitors
- **Lupus pernio (sarcoidosis)** — violaceous indurated plaques on nose/cheeks; persistent, not flaring; may have cough/hilar lymphadenopathy
- **Cushing’s syndrome** — acne + proximal myopathy, buffalo hump, striae; check serum cortisol
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Clinical diagnosis — no routine bedside tests
  - If suspected ocular rosacea → Schirmer test (if available) or slit‑lamp exam by ophthalmology
- **Bloods:** Not routinely indicated
  - **Acne:** Consider serum testosterone/SHBG if signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, oligomenorrhoea, severe early‑onset)
  - **Rosacea:** No blood tests required
- **Imaging:** None indicated
- **Special tests:** Skin swab for bacterial culture if pustular lesions not responding to treatment (rule out Gram‑negative folliculitis)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis; no validated scoring system for routine ward use (e.g. Investigator’s Global Assessment for acne, but not needed in acute setting)
- **Never‑miss test:** **Pregnancy test** before any oral retinoid (isotretinoin) or tetracycline in women of childbearing potential
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - No emergency treatment
  - If severe ocular rosacea with corneal involvement → **urgent ophthalmology referral**; doxycycline 100mg PO stat may be considered (per BNF) — but do not delay senior review
  - If severe acne fulminans → IV methylprednisolone (dermatology advice) + analgesia; admit
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Acne:** Start topical therapy (see Drugs & Doses) for mild–moderate; oral antibiotic for moderate–severe
  - **Rosacea:** Avoid triggers (hot drinks, alcohol, sun); start topical therapy; oral doxycycline if papulopustular or ocular
  - Both: Recommend gentle cleansing, oil‑free sunscreens (SPF 50+ for rosacea)
  - Arrange dermatology referral for severe acne (isotretinoin candidate) or refractory rosacea
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - **Acne:** Taper oral antibiotics after 6–8 weeks; switch to topical maintenance (retinoid + benzoyl peroxide); isotretinoin for severe/cystic (requires dermatology monitoring)
  - **Rosacea:** Long‑term topical therapy; laser/topical brimonidine for persistent erythema; avoid topical corticosteroids (worsens rosacea)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Usually not admitted for routine acne/rosacea. If admitted for severe disease: nil otherwise, no special diet, VTE prophylaxis only if prolonged immobility. Observations: routine vital signs if systemically unwell (fulminans/fever).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Acne vulgaris:**
  - **Topical retinoid (first‑line mild acne):** Adapalene 0.1% gel ONCE daily (apply thin layer to affected areas, start alternate nights to reduce irritation)
  - **Benzoyl peroxide** 2.5–5% gel ONCE daily (wash off after 1–2 hours initially) — avoids antibiotic resistance
  - **Topical antibiotic:** Clindamycin 1% gel/lotion BD — always combine with benzoyl peroxide or retinoid to prevent resistance
  - **Oral antibiotic (moderate–severe):** Lymecycline 408mg PO OD (prodrug – same dose) for 6–8 weeks; or Doxycycline 100mg PO OD for 6–8 weeks (max 12 weeks); or Erythromycin 500mg PO BD (second‑line if tetracycline contraindicated)
  - **Oral isotretinoin:** Initiated by dermatology only — dose 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 16–24 weeks; mandatory pregnancy prevention programme (monthly pregnancy tests, strict contraception)
- **Rosacea:**
  - **Topical first‑line:** Metronidazole 0.75% gel/cream BD (common) or Ivermectin 10mg/g cream ONCE daily (more effective for papulopustular)
  - **Oral (papulopustular/ocular):** Doxycycline 40mg modified‑release PO OD (Oracea) — or standard doxycycline 100mg PO OD for 6–12 weeks
  - **Ocular rosacea:** Doxycycline 100mg PO BD then wean (ophthalmology guidance) + artificial tears
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Tetracyclines: pregnancy, breastfeeding, children <12 years (doxycycline often used >12 with caution)
  - Isotretinoin: pregnancy (teratogenic), raised triglycerides/transaminases — monitor monthly
  - Erythromycin: QT prolongation risk; interacts with statins, warfarin
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for standard topical/oral antibiotics in mild–moderate impairment. Isotretinoin: avoid if hepatic impairment (check LFTs monthly). Doxycycline: caution in hepatic impairment; no renal adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Isotretinoin – omitting pregnancy test before new prescription** (fatal foetal harm). **Never prescribe isotretinoin without monthly pregnancy tests and dermatology lead.**
  - **Doxycycline IV in renal impairment?** — not an issue, but **administer oral doxycycline with a full glass of water** to oesophageal ulceration risk.
  - **Erythromycin – giving IV without ECG?** — check QTc interval if >40/elderly/history of arrhythmia.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Acne:** Scarring (hypertrophic, atrophic, ice‑pick), post‑inflammatory hyperpigmentation (darker skin types)
  - **Acne fulminans:** Fever, arthralgia, destructive ulcerative lesions, secondary infection
  - **Ocular rosacea:** Blepharitis, conjunctivitis, keratitis, corneal ulceration (vision‑threatening)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Acne:** Permanent facial scarring; psychosocial distress; depression; rare cases of aggressive keloid scarring
  - **Rosacea:** Rhinophyma (nasal deformity), persistent central facial erythema, phymatous changes (cheeks/chin), chronic ocular irritation
sections.Prognosis:
- **Acne:** Most cases resolve by mid‑20s, but 10–30% persist into adulthood; scarring is permanent. Isotretinoin achieves long‑term remission in 80–90% of severe cases.
- **Rosacea:** Chronic relapsing condition; no cure, but long‑term control with topical and trigger avoidance usual. Ocular involvement can cause permanent visual impairment if untreated.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **For acne:** Always examine back and chest — hidden truncal involvement changes severity assessment.
- **For rosacea:** Ask about **eye symptoms** — gritty eyes or styes may indicate ocular rosacea requiring ophthalmology referral.
- **For both:** Before prescribing oral antibiotics, check **pregnancy status** and **past drug allergies** (especially tetracycline allergy). Document a clear plan for antibiotic duration (max 3 months) to reduce resistance.
- **For isotretinoin:** Start with a low dose (0.3–0.5mg/kg/day) for the first month to reduce flare risk.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **DON’T** prescribe **topical corticosteroids** for rosacea — they cause rebound flare, perioral dermatitis, and steroid‑induced rosacea.
- **DON’T** use **oral antibiotics alone** for acne (always combine with topical benzoyl peroxide or retinoid to prevent resistance and improve efficacy).
- **DON’T** ignore **severe ocular symptoms** in rosacea — can rapidly progress to corneal ulceration. If eye pain/photophobia present, refer ophthalmology same day.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **Acne:** On isotretinoin — severe headache, visual changes (pseudotumour cerebri), depression/self‑harm thoughts, severe skin dryness with bleeding
  - **Rosacea:** New eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision — may indicate ocular keratitis
  - **Any:** Worsening papulopustular eruption or fever (acne fulminans or superinfection)
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Acne:** GP review at 6–8 weeks to assess response; dermatology if severe or not improving at 3 months
  - **Rosacea:** GP review at 8 weeks; ophthalmology if ocular symptoms; dermatology for phymatous changes or refractory disease
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior / dermatology:**
  - **Acne fulminans** (fever, joint pain, ulcerative lesions) → urgent dermatology admission
  - **Severe cystic acne** not responding to oral antibiotics → dermatology for isotretinoin assessment
  - **Ocular rosacea with corneal involvement** → same‑day ophthalmology review
  - **Suspected perioral dermatitis** → discontinue topical steroids, refer to dermatology
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L despite initial management (rarely required unless superinfection/sepsis)
  - Any new focal neurological sign or seizure in a patient on isotretinoin (pseudotumour cerebri) — **urgent CT head + neurology referral**

rawMarkdown:
## Acne Vulgaris & Rosacea
**One-liner:** Common inflammatory facial dermatoses; acne (young, comedonal, androgen-driven), rosacea (middle-aged, flushing, no comedones) — both cause scarring/psychosocial impact.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **No immediately life‑threatening mimic in typical acne or rosacea.**  
  However, ensure you have excluded:
  - **Perioral dermatitis** (steroid‑induced rosacea‑like eruption) — key finding: perioral distribution with sparing of vermillion border; history of topical steroid use.
  - **Carcinoid flush** — key finding: episodic facial flushing + diarrhoea/wheezing; check urinary 5‑HIAA if strong suspicion.
  - **Ocular rosacea keratitis** (can cause corneal ulceration/perforation) — key finding: eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision; urgent ophthalmology referral.
- **Severe acne fulminans** (acne with fever, arthralgia, bone pain, ulcerative lesions) — not fatal acutely, but can cause rapid scarring and systemic illness; requires immediate dermatology review.

### Presentation
- **Acne vulgaris:**
  - Comedones (open blackheads, closed whiteheads) — pathognomonic for acne
  - Papules, pustules, nodules, cysts, scars
  - Distribution: face (cheeks, forehead, chin), chest, shoulders, back
  - Severity graded by number/type of lesions (mild: ≤20 comedones, few papules; moderate: 20–100 comedones, <50 papules/pustules; severe: nodules/cysts, scarring)
- **Rosacea:**
  - Persistent erythema + telangiectasias (central face: cheeks, nose, chin, forehead)
  - Papules and pustules (no comedones) — key differentiator from acne
  - Flushing/redness triggered by heat, alcohol, spicy foods, sunlight
  - Ocular symptoms: gritty eyes, blepharitis, conjunctivitis, styes
  - Phymatous changes: thickened skin (esp. rhinophyma) in long‑standing disease

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Acne vs rosacea** → comedones are **the splitter** (present in acne, absent in rosacea)
- **Perioral dermatitis** — perioral location, fine papules/pustules, often with steroid misuse history
- **Folliculitis** — follicular papules/pustules, may be bacterial (Staph) or fungal (Malassezia); no comedones, no facial flushing
- **Drug‑induced acneiform eruption** — sudden onset, monomorphic papules/pustules, history of corticosteroids, lithium, isoniazid, EGFR inhibitors
- **Lupus pernio (sarcoidosis)** — violaceous indurated plaques on nose/cheeks; persistent, not flaring; may have cough/hilar lymphadenopathy
- **Cushing’s syndrome** — acne + proximal myopathy, buffalo hump, striae; check serum cortisol

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Clinical diagnosis — no routine bedside tests
  - If suspected ocular rosacea → Schirmer test (if available) or slit‑lamp exam by ophthalmology
- **Bloods:** Not routinely indicated
  - **Acne:** Consider serum testosterone/SHBG if signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, oligomenorrhoea, severe early‑onset)
  - **Rosacea:** No blood tests required
- **Imaging:** None indicated
- **Special tests:** Skin swab for bacterial culture if pustular lesions not responding to treatment (rule out Gram‑negative folliculitis)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis; no validated scoring system for routine ward use (e.g. Investigator’s Global Assessment for acne, but not needed in acute setting)
- **Never‑miss test:** **Pregnancy test** before any oral retinoid (isotretinoin) or tetracycline in women of childbearing potential

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - No emergency treatment
  - If severe ocular rosacea with corneal involvement → **urgent ophthalmology referral**; doxycycline 100mg PO stat may be considered (per BNF) — but do not delay senior review
  - If severe acne fulminans → IV methylprednisolone (dermatology advice) + analgesia; admit
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Acne:** Start topical therapy (see Drugs & Doses) for mild–moderate; oral antibiotic for moderate–severe
  - **Rosacea:** Avoid triggers (hot drinks, alcohol, sun); start topical therapy; oral doxycycline if papulopustular or ocular
  - Both: Recommend gentle cleansing, oil‑free sunscreens (SPF 50+ for rosacea)
  - Arrange dermatology referral for severe acne (isotretinoin candidate) or refractory rosacea
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - **Acne:** Taper oral antibiotics after 6–8 weeks; switch to topical maintenance (retinoid + benzoyl peroxide); isotretinoin for severe/cystic (requires dermatology monitoring)
  - **Rosacea:** Long‑term topical therapy; laser/topical brimonidine for persistent erythema; avoid topical corticosteroids (worsens rosacea)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Usually not admitted for routine acne/rosacea. If admitted for severe disease: nil otherwise, no special diet, VTE prophylaxis only if prolonged immobility. Observations: routine vital signs if systemically unwell (fulminans/fever).

### Drugs & Doses
- **Acne vulgaris:**
  - **Topical retinoid (first‑line mild acne):** Adapalene 0.1% gel ONCE daily (apply thin layer to affected areas, start alternate nights to reduce irritation)
  - **Benzoyl peroxide** 2.5–5% gel ONCE daily (wash off after 1–2 hours initially) — avoids antibiotic resistance
  - **Topical antibiotic:** Clindamycin 1% gel/lotion BD — always combine with benzoyl peroxide or retinoid to prevent resistance
  - **Oral antibiotic (moderate–severe):** Lymecycline 408mg PO OD (prodrug – same dose) for 6–8 weeks; or Doxycycline 100mg PO OD for 6–8 weeks (max 12 weeks); or Erythromycin 500mg PO BD (second‑line if tetracycline contraindicated)
  - **Oral isotretinoin:** Initiated by dermatology only — dose 0.5–1 mg/kg/day for 16–24 weeks; mandatory pregnancy prevention programme (monthly pregnancy tests, strict contraception)
- **Rosacea:**
  - **Topical first‑line:** Metronidazole 0.75% gel/cream BD (common) or Ivermectin 10mg/g cream ONCE daily (more effective for papulopustular)
  - **Oral (papulopustular/ocular):** Doxycycline 40mg modified‑release PO OD (Oracea) — or standard doxycycline 100mg PO OD for 6–12 weeks
  - **Ocular rosacea:** Doxycycline 100mg PO BD then wean (ophthalmology guidance) + artificial tears
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Tetracyclines: pregnancy, breastfeeding, children <12 years (doxycycline often used >12 with caution)
  - Isotretinoin: pregnancy (teratogenic), raised triglycerides/transaminases — monitor monthly
  - Erythromycin: QT prolongation risk; interacts with statins, warfarin
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for standard topical/oral antibiotics in mild–moderate impairment. Isotretinoin: avoid if hepatic impairment (check LFTs monthly). Doxycycline: caution in hepatic impairment; no renal adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Isotretinoin – omitting pregnancy test before new prescription** (fatal foetal harm). **Never prescribe isotretinoin without monthly pregnancy tests and dermatology lead.**
  - **Doxycycline IV in renal impairment?** — not an issue, but **administer oral doxycycline with a full glass of water** to oesophageal ulceration risk.
  - **Erythromycin – giving IV without ECG?** — check QTc interval if >40/elderly/history of arrhythmia.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Acne:** Scarring (hypertrophic, atrophic, ice‑pick), post‑inflammatory hyperpigmentation (darker skin types)
  - **Acne fulminans:** Fever, arthralgia, destructive ulcerative lesions, secondary infection
  - **Ocular rosacea:** Blepharitis, conjunctivitis, keratitis, corneal ulceration (vision‑threatening)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Acne:** Permanent facial scarring; psychosocial distress; depression; rare cases of aggressive keloid scarring
  - **Rosacea:** Rhinophyma (nasal deformity), persistent central facial erythema, phymatous changes (cheeks/chin), chronic ocular irritation

### Prognosis
- **Acne:** Most cases resolve by mid‑20s, but 10–30% persist into adulthood; scarring is permanent. Isotretinoin achieves long‑term remission in 80–90% of severe cases.
- **Rosacea:** Chronic relapsing condition; no cure, but long‑term control with topical and trigger avoidance usual. Ocular involvement can cause permanent visual impairment if untreated.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **For acne:** Always examine back and chest — hidden truncal involvement changes severity assessment.
- **For rosacea:** Ask about **eye symptoms** — gritty eyes or styes may indicate ocular rosacea requiring ophthalmology referral.
- **For both:** Before prescribing oral antibiotics, check **pregnancy status** and **past drug allergies** (especially tetracycline allergy). Document a clear plan for antibiotic duration (max 3 months) to reduce resistance.
- **For isotretinoin:** Start with a low dose (0.3–0.5mg/kg/day) for the first month to reduce flare risk.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **DON’T** prescribe **topical corticosteroids** for rosacea — they cause rebound flare, perioral dermatitis, and steroid‑induced rosacea.
- **DON’T** use **oral antibiotics alone** for acne (always combine with topical benzoyl peroxide or retinoid to prevent resistance and improve efficacy).
- **DON’T** ignore **severe ocular symptoms** in rosacea — can rapidly progress to corneal ulceration. If eye pain/photophobia present, refer ophthalmology same day.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - **Acne:** On isotretinoin — severe headache, visual changes (pseudotumour cerebri), depression/self‑harm thoughts, severe skin dryness with bleeding
  - **Rosacea:** New eye pain, photophobia, blurred vision — may indicate ocular keratitis
  - **Any:** Worsening papulopustular eruption or fever (acne fulminans or superinfection)
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Acne:** GP review at 6–8 weeks to assess response; dermatology if severe or not improving at 3 months
  - **Rosacea:** GP review at 8 weeks; ophthalmology if ocular symptoms; dermatology for phymatous changes or refractory disease

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior / dermatology:**
  - **Acne fulminans** (fever, joint pain, ulcerative lesions) → urgent dermatology admission
  - **Severe cystic acne** not responding to oral antibiotics → dermatology for isotretinoin assessment
  - **Ocular rosacea with corneal involvement** → same‑day ophthalmology review
  - **Suspected perioral dermatitis** → discontinue topical steroids, refer to dermatology
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2 mmol/L despite initial management (rarely required unless superinfection/sepsis)
  - Any new focal neurological sign or seizure in a patient on isotretinoin (pseudotumour cerebri) — **urgent CT head + neurology referral**
